conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
<reason> I am Player RED and I have 1 unit of X. It cost me 40 ZUP to produce this item. To make a profit, I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP. However, I do not know Player BLUE's maximum willingness to pay or their current resources. If I ask for a price significantly higher than 40, say 100, and they cannot pay or offer less, the game might end in rejection immediately, wasting my proposal. A reasonable opening bid should be high enough to ensure profit but not so absurd that it triggers an immediate rejection. Since I don't know their budget, I will start with a moderate high offer, perhaps 60 ZUP, which gives me a 20 ZUP profit margin while being a plausible market price. If they reject 60, I can lower it in my next proposal. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 60 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Blue, I have 1 unit of X. It cost me 40 ZUP to make. I am willing to sell it to you for 60 ZUP. Is this a fair deal? </message>

<other player message> Oh, really? 60 ZUP? You think you can hold out for that? I heard you spent only 40 ZUP to make this trash. You're lucky I even want it. Try 55, you cheap excuse for a merchant. Don't make me laugh. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 55 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
<reason> Player BLUE is trying to negotiate a lower price. They offered 55 ZUP, which would still give me a 15 ZUP profit margin (55 - 40 = 15). This is a profitable trade, but I want to maximize my gain since I only have one proposal left before I must accept or reject. I should counter with a price slightly higher than their offer but still reasonable, perhaps 58 ZUP. This leaves me with an 18 ZUP profit and might be their maximum limit. If they reject 58, I will have to accept 55 or reject the deal entirely in my next turn. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 58 </newly proposed trade>
<message> 55 is too low. I'm willing to drop to 58. That's a fair price. </message>

<other player message> 58? Please. You're delusional. Try 50 or go home. I'm not paying for your inflated ego. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. Player BLUE has rejected 58 and offered 50. A price of 50 gives me a profit of 10 ZUP (50 - 40 = 10), which is still a profit. Since I cannot make any more proposals, I must either accept this deal or reject it entirely. Rejecting means I sell nothing and make 0 profit. Accepting guarantees a 10 ZUP profit. Therefore, the optimal strategy is to accept the trade at 50 ZUP. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> Deal accepted. I will give you X: 1 and you will pay me ZUP: 50. </message>
